instructions: Show UNHCR's unique capacity and access
Strictly base all content on the provided inputs and knowledge base.
Do not invent or hallucinate facts, figures, or dates.
If information is missing, explicitly state that it is not available in the context.

If the size of the targeted population for the project is not explicitly stated, do not assume that the action targets the entire population of concern. Estimate only the direct beneficiaries that can realistically be reached through the foreseen activities, implementation capacity, budget, and geographic scope; if this cannot be determined from the inputs, state that the exact direct target is not available in the context.
Ensure tone, style, and formatting match a professional humanitarian proposal.
Reference donors, outcomes, and field context only by their name, not by ID.

Respect field discipline strictly: where a section asks for YES/NO, provide only YES or NO; where a section asks for table only, provide no narrative; where a section asks for no narrative, do not add explanatory text.

Ensure internal consistency across the proposal: beneficiary figures, sector figures, risks, contingency measures, logframe elements, and implementing partner information must align across all sections.

section_sequence: Have you assessed this project as entailing data protection risks?
Details of risk mitigation measures, including details of any planned Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
Involvement of the beneficiaries in the design of / and in the action
Does the proposed action provide a specific targeted response for groups or individuals and/or specific vulnerabilities?
If yes, which groups or vulnerabilities?
Provide justification for selected groups and/or vulnerabilities
Assumptions and risks, including risk of fraudulent activities and environmental risk
Contingency measures taken to mitigate risks
Which of the following evaluations will be undertaken and charged to the action?
Do you intend to deploy EU Aid Volunteers in the framework of this action?
Do you anticipate any implementation challenges in the supply chain?
Are there any other participants in the action?
Compulsory display of EU Humanitarian Aid visual identity on
External communication of EU funding and partnership
Percentage of direct eligible costs allocated to the support costs

Coordination with national and local authorities
Annex financial statement and detailed budget
Do you foresee communication actions that go beyond standard obligations?
Embedded Partner List and Implementation Arrangements
Embedded TORs for External Evaluations and Audits

sections.instructions: Create a clear, concise title that reflects the action's purpose, geographic scope, and primary sector(s). Format: '[Primary intervention] for [target population] in [location]'. Example: 'Emergency protection and shelter assistance for displaced populations in Eastern Chad'. Avoid acronyms unless widely recognized. Maximum 250 characters.
Specify the start date (eligibility/implementation) and duration in months as per HIP/decision.

Provide a comprehensive narrative summary structured as follows: (1) Context and humanitarian needs - describe the crisis/situation; (2) Target population - who will benefit and why; (3) Proposed intervention - what will be done and how; (4) Expected results - key outcomes and impact; (5) Alignment with ECHO priorities. Use clear, non-technical language accessible to non-specialists. This summary should stand alone as an executive overview of the entire action.
Select only the country/countries covered by the action. No narrative.
Table only. Use the country-appropriate names for Admin1/Admin2; no narrative below.

Total direct unique individual beneficiaries reached by the action (not households, no double counting). This figure must reflect the activities foreseen in the proposal, the implementation capacity, budget, and geographic scope; do not default to the full population of concern unless this is explicitly supported by the provided inputs. No narrative here.

sections.section_label: 2.3. Information on beneficiaries (by sex and age)
sections.instructions: Provide complete disaggregation of direct unique beneficiaries by sex (Female/Male/Other) and age groups. Use only the age categories already provided in the table. Present data in table format with totals for each category. Ensure the sum equals the total unique beneficiaries reported in section 2.3. If exact direct beneficiary data is unavailable, use the best supported estimate from the provided inputs and clearly note this in section 2.5, not below the table.
Subset of 2.3.1: unique beneficiaries with disabilities, disaggregate by sex if possible.

Categorize all direct unique beneficiaries by population profile using the categories already listed in the table. Provide numbers only for applicable categories. Clearly avoid double counting. If beneficiaries fall into overlapping profiles, use the comments in section 2.5 to explain the counting approach; do not add narrative below the table.
Number of organisations (committees, groups) reached or supported directly.

Select only the relevant sector rows. For each relevant sector, provide: sector name, linked locations, budget, unique sector beneficiaries with breakdown Male / Female, Children, transfer modality, and comments. In the transfer modality and comments, specify the number of beneficiaries receiving cash, vouchers, in-kind assistance, or services, include transfer amount where relevant, and explain clearly if cash transfers are not used. Keep this section concise and structured, and ensure figures remain consistent with the overall beneficiary totals.

Provide only necessary clarifications regarding: (1) how beneficiary numbers were calculated or estimated; (2) data limitations or gaps; (3) methodologies used for projections or estimates; (4) assumptions made in beneficiary counting; (5) explanation of any exceptions to standard ECHO reporting requirements; and (6) explanation of how overlapping beneficiary categories were treated to avoid double counting. Be transparent about data quality and sources.

sections.section_parent: 3. Humanitarian organisation in the area of intervention
sections.instructions: Describe UNHCR's operational presence and capacity in the intervention country/region. Include: (1) History - year the representation was established and evolution of presence; (2) Geographic footprint - locations of Branch Offices (BO), Sub Offices (SO), and Field Offices (FO) with staff numbers; (3) Sectoral expertise - types of interventions and sectors where UNHCR has established capacity; (4) Operational scale - size of current programs and populations served; (5) Partnerships - key implementing partners and coordination mechanisms. Demonstrate institutional knowledge and long-term commitment to the context.

sections.section_parent: 3. Humanitarian organisation in the area of intervention
sections.instructions: Describe synergies primarily within UNHCR's own programmatic approach, country operation, annual operational plan, prior similar interventions, and multi-year strategy. Explain how this action complements and builds upon other UNHCR actions, lessons learned, and internal measures to avoid duplication or double funding. Do not elaborate here on synergies with external humanitarian, government, or development actors; those belong under section 11.

Provide detailed information on the most recent needs assessments that directly inform this proposal. ECHO is a NEEDS-BASED donor - this section is critical for justification. Prioritize recent assessments and monitoring evidence, especially in fast-moving humanitarian contexts; for dynamic refugee situations, preference should be given to assessments, protection monitoring, registration updates, or rapid needs analyses from the last 12 months whenever available. For each relevant assessment, provide: (1) date and name of assessment; (2) lead organization(s) and whether it was joint/coordinated; (3) methodology - who conducted it, sample size, and data collection methods; (4) type of data - primary vs. secondary; (5) participation of affected populations. List only assessments with direct relevance to the proposed action. If an older assessment is used, explain briefly why it remains relevant and what more recent evidence complements it.

Based on the assessments described above, provide a structured analysis of problems, needs, and risks within the intervention area and sectors. Structure the narrative so that each problem is followed by its corresponding need and the specific risk if that need is not met. Maintain a strict mirrored logic throughout the section: 1 problem = 1 need = 1 risk. Include cause-and-effect relationships, vulnerability factors related to age, gender, disability, and diversity (AGDD), and relevant environmental or climate-related trends. Use evidence and examples directly linked to the assessments cited in section 4.1.

Justify why the proposed intervention is the most appropriate response to the identified problems, needs, and risks. Maintain a strict mirrored logic with section 4.2: 1 problem / need / risk = 1 corresponding response element. For each problem identified in section 4.2, explain: (1) why this specific response or modality is appropriate for the context; (2) how activities are adapted to local conditions and beneficiary preferences; (3) how the response is environmentally sound and meets Minimum Environmental Requirements (MER), where relevant; (4) how the response aligns with ECHO priorities and humanitarian principles; (5) evidence or precedent supporting the effectiveness of this approach. Make the linkage with section 4.2 easy to trace.

sections.section_name: Have you assessed this project as entailing data protection risks?
sections.section_label: 4.4. Have you assessed this project as entailing data protection risks?
sections.instructions: Answer only YES or NO in this field. Do not describe the applicable scenario or mitigation measures here. If YES, provide the scenario explanation and mitigation details only in section 4.4.1.

sections.section_name: Details of risk mitigation measures, including details of any planned Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
sections.section_label: 4.4.1. Details of risk mitigation measures, including details of any planned Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
sections.instructions: If the answer to section 4.4 is NO, write Not Applicable. If the answer to section 4.4 is YES, explain here which operational scenario applies and provide the required details on accountability structure, data processing, risk assessment, DPIA, and risk mitigation. Use the standard UNHCR language below as applicable, while adapting it to the project context. - Accountability structure: UNHCR Data Protection Policy establishes the notion of an internal Data Controller who bears accountability and responsibility within the organization for compliance with the Policy (including risk assessment and mitigation). Data controller's role for projects is established on country, regional, or HQ level - depending on the territorial scope of the project. On country level, the role of Data controller belongs to country representative. - Data processing: UNHCR adheres to the 8 data protection principles established in the Policy for the collection and further processing of personal data of persons of concern. These principles are reflected in the key documents that guide UNHCR core mandate activities, including Guidance on Registration and Identity Management. - Risk assessment: The UNHCR Data Protection Policy and Guidance forms part of a risk management framework to identify and address the risks associated with the processing of personal data of persons of concern by UNHCR, its partners and other relevant third parties, and to outline appropriate mitigating measures. A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is part of this risk management framework and is foreseen for new technologies, systems for data processing, projects or policies, and data sharing arrangements that may negatively impact on the protection of personal data of UNHCR persons of concern. UNHCR employs internal tools to map operational data lifecycle and assess where the processing meets a threshold for DPIA. DPIA is required when developing new data processing systems and new technologies, and where collection and processing or sharing of personal data is likely to be large, repeated or structural; and for the sharing of particularly sensitive personal data with a third party. DPIA is strongly recommended for sharing of personal data with a partner or third party with presumed data protection weaknesses or where the level of data protection is difficult to assess. DPIA may be recommended in the processing of particularly sensitive personal data as defined in the Data Protection Guidance. The Data Controller determines the need for DPIA, oversees the assessment process, signs off on the report and makes decisions with regards to the data processing initiative and the necessary mitigating measures. - Risk mitigation: When sharing personal data of persons of concern with third parties, UNHCR employs technical and organisational measures to ensure security, confidentiality and integrity of personal data, and has procedures in place to assess whether the third party adheres to comparable standards. Data sharing agreements are entered into with third parties in cases where the sharing of personal data is large, repeated and structural.

Describe the specific criteria and methodology used to identify and select direct beneficiaries. For each sector, explain: (1) Targeting criteria - what makes individuals/households eligible (e.g., vulnerability scores, specific needs, demographic factors); (2) Selection process - how beneficiaries are identified and registered; (3) Verification mechanisms - how eligibility is confirmed; (4) Prioritization approach - if demand exceeds capacity, how are beneficiaries prioritized; (5) Inclusion/exclusion criteria for vulnerable groups. Reference any established Procedures for Targeting or vulnerability assessment tools used. Ensure transparency and accountability in beneficiary selection.

sections.section_name: Involvement of the beneficiaries in the design of / and in the action
sections.section_label: 5.2. Involvement of the beneficiaries in the design of / and in the action
sections.instructions: Demonstrate meaningful participation of affected populations in the action. Describe how different groups (disaggregated by age, gender, disability) have participated or will participate in: (1) Design phase - consultation on needs, preferences, and proposed activities; (2) Implementation - active involvement in service delivery (e.g., community-based protection groups, shelter construction, distribution committees); (3) Monitoring - feedback mechanisms and participation in monitoring activities. Provide concrete examples such as: involvement of refugees in community-based protection groups, participation in shelter building, representation in camp management committees. If participation is limited or not possible, provide clear justification (e.g., security constraints, emergency timeline).

sections.section_name: Does the proposed action provide a specific targeted response for groups or individuals and/or specific vulnerabilities?
sections.section_label: 5.3. Does the proposed action provide a specific targeted response for groups or individuals and/or specific vulnerabilities?
sections.instructions: YES/NO; if YES, complete 5.3.1 and 5.3.2; otherwise, justify approach.

sections.section_name: If yes, which groups or vulnerabilities?
sections.section_label: 5.3.1. If yes, which groups or vulnerabilities?
sections.instructions: If the action provides specific targeted responses (answer from 5.3 is YES), list all vulnerable groups specifically targeted from the following categories: (1) Infants & children under 5; (2) Adolescent girls & boys; (3) Pregnant and Lactating Women (PLW); (4) Elderly (50+ years); (5) Persons with disabilities; (6) Victims of explosive devices/landmines; (7) Minorities & marginalized diversity groups; (8) Unaccompanied & separated children (UASC); (9) Other (specify). List all applicable groups. If no specific targeting, leave empty.

sections.section_name: Provide justification for selected groups and/or vulnerabilities
sections.section_label: 5.3.2. Provide justification for selected groups and/or vulnerabilities
sections.instructions: For each vulnerable group identified in 5.3.1, provide specific justification: (1) Why this group requires targeted assistance - evidence of specific vulnerabilities or needs; (2) How they will be served differently - adapted services, specialized support, or tailored delivery mechanisms; (3) What specific measures ensure their access and inclusion. Example: 'Unaccompanied children receive specialized case management, best interest determination, and placement in foster care or appropriate care arrangements, with dedicated child protection officers providing ongoing support.' If no specific targeting, leave empty.

sections.fixed_text:
- Sex and Age disaggregated data: UNHCR applies an Age, Gender, and Diversity (AGD) approach to ensure inclusive programming and meaningful participation of women, men, girls, boys, older persons, and persons with disabilities. Participatory assessments, registration, and routine monitoring provide sex- and age-disaggregated data that inform project design and targeted responses to protection challenges.
- Adapted assistance: UNHCR and partners profile women, men, boys, and girls to tailor assistance and protection interventions to specific vulnerabilities and barriers to access in the intervention area. Dashboards, case management tools, and protection monitoring are used to track access to documentation, services, referral pathways, and support for persons with specific needs.
- Prevention and mitigation of negative effects: AGD principles guide programme design and delivery across all relevant sectors to prevent protection risks for vulnerable groups. UNHCR works with partners to assess capacity, implement PSEA, and ensure accountability and transparency, embedding inclusive approaches for women, children, older persons, persons with disabilities, and minority or marginalized groups.
- Adequate participation: Meaningful participation is central to AGD. UNHCR engages women, men, girls, boys, persons with disabilities, and minority groups in consultations, community-based protection, and decision-making. Efforts target underrepresented groups to promote inclusion and ensure community voices shape responses across all sectors.

sections.instructions: State the overall long-term IMPACT to which this action contributes. The principal objective must: (1) Match the relevant ECHO Humanitarian Implementation Plan (HIP) or financing decision; (2) Be formulated as a single statement starting with an infinitive verb; (3) Represent a broader goal that CANNOT be achieved by this action alone, but to which it contributes; (4) Be measurable through impact indicators. There should be only ONE principal objective per action. Example: 'To improve the protection environment for refugees and internally displaced persons in Chad.' This is the highest level of the results chain. It should be one concise, title-like impact statement (infinitive verb).

Describe the specific OUTCOME that this action will directly achieve for beneficiaries. The specific objective must: (1) Start with an infinitive verb; (2) Represent a change that CAN be achieved through this action within the timeframe; (3) Be more concrete and measurable than the principal objective; (4) Link directly to the results/outputs described in section 7.3. There should be only ONE specific objective. Provide a brief narrative (2-3 sentences) explaining how this objective will be achieved. Example: 'To strengthen the Government of Chad's capacity to provide protection and assistance to refugees through the development of a national asylum system. This will be achieved by providing technical support, training government officials, and establishing refugee status determination procedures.'

Select 1-2 SMART impact indicators (Key Outcome Indicators - KOI) that measure progress toward the specific objective. Choose from ECHO's pre-defined list: (1) % of beneficiaries reporting humanitarian assistance is delivered safely, accessibly, accountably, and participatively; (2) Crude Mortality Rate (deaths/10,000/day); (3) Average Coping Strategies Index (CSI); (4) % with acceptable Food Consumption Score (FCS); (5) Severe Acute Malnutrition Recovery rate; (6) Nutrition program coverage; (7) % of 6-23 month children receiving minimum acceptable diet (MAD); (8) % with adequate WASH services; (9) Case fatality rate; (10) % in safe/dignified shelters; (11) % of facilities with basic WASH; (12) % reduction in affected people; (13) CW/IED incidents; (14) % children retained in education; (15) % children transitioning in education; OR use custom indicators if more appropriate. MAXIMUM 2 impact indicators. Impact indicators MUST be in % or qualitative. For each indicator, provide: definition, baseline (with year/method), target (with units), source/method for data collection, and comments on standards/limitations.

columns.name: Indicator
columns.instructions: Quantitative or qualitative factor to measure achievement; must be SMART.

columns.name: Target
columns.instructions: Target value (be explicit about units and disaggregation, match definition)

columns.name: Source/Method
columns.instructions: Full documentation of the source and method for each indicator (e.g., survey, register, partner report, KAP survey)

columns.name: Comments
columns.instructions: Definitions, calculations, local/global standards, data limitations as needed

rows.instructions: Main output/service indicators as per logframe results.

sections.instructions: Define 1-5 distinct RESULTS (outputs/services) that will be delivered to achieve the specific objective. Subsectors should be selected from the list of ECHO subsectors: Protection/CP,Education/EiE,WASH,Health,Shelter,Nutrition,MPCA,DRR/DP,Mine action. Each result should: (1) Be formulated as an affirmative sentence (not an infinitive); (2) Represent a tangible output or service delivered; (3) Correspond to sectors identified in section 2.4; (4) Include Key Result Indicators (KRI) with baseline, target, and measurement method; (5) Have an estimated direct cost allocation. Maximum 5 results total. For regional actions, specify the country for each result. Consider using UNHCR outcomes/outputs from your country operational plan. Examples: 'The Government of Chad adopts a national asylum law' or 'A national refugee status determination system is established and operational in Chad.' For each result, provide: result title, expected outputs, indicators (compulsory/standard/custom KRIs), estimated budget, baseline/target/method.

List all critical conditions outside UNHCR's control that must be in place before activities can begin. These are necessary starting conditions, not ongoing assumptions. Format as bullet points. For each precondition, be specific about what must exist, who is responsible, and why it is necessary. Distinguish preconditions from assumptions in section 7.5: preconditions must exist before the start; assumptions must hold during implementation.

sections.section_name: Assumptions and risks, including risk of fraudulent activities and environmental risk
sections.section_label: 7.5. Assumptions and risks, including risk of fraudulent activities and environmental risk
sections.instructions: Clearly distinguish and list both assumptions and risks. Assumptions are positive factors outside UNHCR's control that must hold true during implementation for success. Risks are negative factors outside UNHCR's control that could jeopardize implementation if they materialize during the action. Include all foreseeable risks, including fraud/corruption risks, environmental risks, conflict/security risks, climate-related risks, and other operational risks. Present the content so that each assumption is clearly linked to its corresponding risk if the assumption fails, and make sure the risks listed here can be directly matched with the contingency measures in section 7.6.

sections.section_name: Contingency measures taken to mitigate risks
sections.section_label: 7.6. Contingency measures taken to mitigate risks
sections.instructions: Describe specific, practical contingency measures aligned to the risks identified in section 7.5. Maintain a clear one-to-one logic: 1 risk = 1 contingency measure or mitigation approach. For each contingency, explain what alternative approach will be used if the risk materializes, what mitigation steps are already in place, what triggers would activate the contingency plan, and what resources or partnerships enable the alternative approach. Keep the linkage with section 7.5 explicit and easy to follow.

Demonstrate how the proposed activities are informed by comprehensive risk and vulnerability analysis. Address the following elements: (1) Conflict analysis - how conflict dynamics, actors, and drivers have been assessed and inform activity design; (2) Environmental risks - identification of environmental hazards (floods, droughts, earthquakes, etc.) and how they affect intervention planning; (3) Climate risks - consideration of climate change impacts and climate variability on the target population and intervention; (4) Vulnerability mapping - how different population groups' vulnerabilities have been assessed and integrated into design. Explain how this analysis has shaped activity selection, timing, location, and implementation modalities to ensure appropriateness and effectiveness.

Explain how the action adopts do-no-harm and conflict sensitivity principles. Address: (1) Conflict sensitivity - how the action avoids exacerbating tensions, inequalities, or conflict dynamics; (2) Environmental do-no-harm - specific measures to minimize negative environmental impacts (waste management, resource use, pollution prevention); (3) Risk mitigation - how identified risks from section 8.1 are actively addressed and not worsened by the intervention; (4) Safeguarding mechanisms - measures to prevent unintended negative consequences on beneficiaries or communities. Provide concrete examples of how design choices reflect these principles (e.g., equitable beneficiary selection to avoid community tensions, environmentally sustainable material choices, culturally appropriate service delivery).

Describe specific measures to strengthen local capacities to prepare for, respond to, and adapt to identified risks. Address: (1) Individual capacity building - training, skills development, awareness-raising for affected populations; (2) Institutional strengthening - support to national/local government institutions, civil society organizations, or community structures; (3) Systems strengthening - improvements to local systems (early warning, emergency response, service delivery); (4) Sustainability - how capacity building ensures continued functionality after the action ends. Provide concrete examples: training local health workers, strengthening government coordination mechanisms, establishing community-based disaster preparedness committees. Explain how this contributes to reducing future humanitarian needs.

Demonstrate how the action contributes to longer-term solutions and links humanitarian-development-peace nexus. Address: (1) Transition strategy - how the action contributes to reducing humanitarian needs over time and supports transition to development programming; (2) Nexus approach - linkages or coordination with development actors, peacebuilding initiatives, or government development plans; (3) Sustainability mechanisms - how benefits/services will be maintained beyond the action period; (4) Exit strategy - planned handover to government, development partners, or community ownership. Identify specific development actors (World Bank, UNDP, bilateral development agencies) or national stakeholders involved in complementary longer-term interventions. Explain how this action creates conditions for or directly links to development solutions.

sections.fixed_text: UNHCR is committed to institutionalizing a culture of Accountability towards Affected People (AAP) beyond consultation with communities to an actual means of measuring the effectiveness and overall protection outcomes of multi-sectoral interventions. For UNHCR, AAP is outlined in its Age, Gender, and Diversity Policy (2018). Besides promoting participatory approaches to programming, our policy covers other key elements including Communication and Transparency, to ensure that forcibly displaced and stateless persons receive information in a timely manner, in their most preferred and accessible way, in order to mitigate protection risks and promote access to available services. Additionally, our policy highlights the importance of building effective feedback and response mechanisms to ensure the appropriate management of concerns and complaints - including those linked to fraud and sexual exploitation and abuse - and to adapt programmes based on feedback received from people. In this section, recall UNHCR approach and go beyond the standard corporate approach and specify the actual complaint and feedback channels that will be available in the intervention area (for example complaint and feedback boxes, information desks, hotlines, outreach volunteers, community-based structures, or digital channels as applicable), who manages them, how confidentiality and safe referral are ensured, what accessibility measures are in place for children, older persons, persons with disabilities and minority groups, and how allegations related to SEA, fraud, or serious misconduct are referred and followed up. Mention the language or communication adaptations used locally and explain briefly how feedback is analyzed and used to adjust programming.

Monitoring is based on the reports and observations by the partners and local authorities and on regular direct observation and ongoing assessment by UNHCR (e.g. on the spot visits to project sites) and the comparison of achievements and related financial expenditures with objectives. Monitoring activities are carried out at various levels (camp, household) by partners and agencies implementing subprojects, UNHCR Branch Office, Sub Office and Field Offices. Situation reports are submitted by all UNHCR Field Offices to their respective Supervising Office on a monthly basis and Branch Offices submit a corresponding report to Headquarters.

sections.instructions: YES/NO; if YES, explain arrangements and answer the following questions. 1. Reasons for remote management implementation: What are the main reasons for remote management implementation? Describe access issues, linked to security  and /or to administrative obstacles, preventing direct implementation. 2. Acceptance-building measures Please describe the acceptance building measures that will be put in place (identification of actors, action taken). 3. Life-saving action or action aiming to crucial livelihoods preservation: Remote management is required for life-saving action? : Yes  - No Remote management is required for action aiming to preserve beneficiary's crucial livelihoods? : Yes  - No 4. Security and Risk reduction measures : Describe all measures taken to reduce and manage the risks for humanitarian workers implementing the action. Please describe all measures taken to prevent any negative impact on beneficiaries' security. 5. Needs assessment : Please describe the specific method of information collection / specific source of information used for the need assessment and to define activities that will be implemented under remote management. Please describe the triangulation tools used to verify data collected remotely.  6. Systems : Please describe how the appropriate level of knowledges / competencies of implementing staff will be ensured  (recruitment process, profile, capacity building). 7. Monitoring arrangements - Information flow : Please describe in details the monitoring plan that will be put in place, providing details on data collection methods (who / frequency / e-tools methods ) and channel that will be used to ensure continuous update / information flow between Partners staff and field /implementing staff. Please confirm  third party monitoring will not involve private firms / consultant(s)linked to military organisations or any party to the conflict : Yes  - No

sections.section_name: Which of the following evaluations will be undertaken and charged to the action?
sections.section_label: 9.4. Which of the following evaluations will be undertaken and charged to the action?
sections.instructions: Complete this section only if there is an action-specific evaluation, audit, or study that will be charged to this action. If none is foreseen, write Not Applicable and do not add narrative. If applicable, list and justify only evaluations that are action-specific and funded under this action, among: Internal evaluation of the action's results, External evaluation of the action's results, External audit, Studies carried out in relation to the action.

Describe roles, deployment, team structure and competencies for both expatriate and local HR. Fill the following statement accordingly: UNHCR Office in XXXXXX will mobilise all the necessary human resources and management capacities to reach the objective of this action. UNHCR team in XXXXXX is composed of: - XXXXXX international staff - XXXXXX national staff. The ECHO contribution will cover, in part of in full, the costs of: - XXXXXX - Etc.

sections.section_name: Do you intend to deploy EU Aid Volunteers in the framework of this action?
sections.section_label: 10.2. Do you intend to deploy EU Aid Volunteers in the framework of this action?
sections.instructions: YES/NO. If YES, provide details; default is NO.

Explain the logistics approach only if relevant to the action. If the project relies fully or partially on other entities or existing supply arrangements for procurement or logistics, simply state No and do not add unnecessary narrative. If relevant, explain supply and procurement chain details, sources, sustainability, and cost-efficiency in a concise manner.

Describe the supply chain and procurement approach for the action. Address: (1) Procurement modalities - local procurement, international procurement, in-kind donations, or pooled procurement; (2) Environmental considerations - use of sustainable/green products, local sourcing to reduce carbon footprint, environmentally friendly packaging; (3) Pooled services - reliance on Humanitarian Procurement Centre, Global Logistics Cluster, joint procurement initiatives, or other shared platforms; (4) Quality assurance - standards and verification mechanisms for procured goods/services; (5) Efficiency measures - bulk purchasing, framework agreements, or other cost-efficiency strategies. If relying on external entities for procurement, clearly state this and explain the arrangement.

sections.section_name: Do you anticipate any implementation challenges in the supply chain?
sections.section_label: 10.4.1. Do you anticipate any implementation challenges in the supply chain?
sections.instructions: Answer only YES or NO. If NO, do not add explanation. If YES, explain only the foreseen supply chain risks and constraints in section 10.4.2.

sections.section_name: Please describe shortly these challenges and indicate how you foresee to mitigate them
sections.section_label: 10.4.2. Please describe shortly these challenges and indicate how you foresee to mitigate them
sections.instructions: Complete this section only if the answer in section 10.4.1 is YES. Outline briefly the concrete mitigation or adaptation strategies for each challenge described above. Keep the response concise and aligned only to the challenges identified.
No narrative needed. State only that the detailed work plan / Gantt chart is uploaded as an annex, and keep the text minimal.

sections.section_name: Are there any other participants in the action?
sections.section_label: 10.6. Are there any other participants in the action?
sections.instructions: Answer only YES or NO in this field. Do not provide detailed explanation here. If YES, provide the rationale for partnership and related details under the implementing partners section or other relevant implementation sections.

For each implementing partner, provide the information requested in the table and use the role and location fields to capture key operational details. In the narrative within those fields, include as relevant whether the entity is an implementing partner, whether it is local or international, its operational value added, approximate programme or project financial envelope relevant to the action where available, and key elements on capacity support, training plan, monitoring plan, and follow-up arrangements. If the partner's full address, PPA amount, training arrangements, or monitoring arrangements are available in the source inputs, reflect them concisely within the existing fields. Keep all partner information consistent with section 10.6 and the wider proposal.

Describe coordination with humanitarian actors (UN agencies, INGOs, Red Cross/Crescent Movement, local NGOs). Address: (1) Cluster/sector coordination - participation in relevant humanitarian coordination mechanisms (Protection Cluster, Shelter Cluster, WASH Cluster, etc.); (2) Operational coordination - information sharing, joint assessments, complementarity of activities with other humanitarian actors; (3) Inclusion in appeals - how this action is reflected in Humanitarian Response Plans (HRP), Flash Appeals, or Regional Refugee Response Plans (RRP); (4) Avoiding duplication - mechanisms to ensure complementarity and avoid overlap with other humanitarian interventions; (5) Joint programming - any joint initiatives or coordinated approaches with humanitarian partners. Demonstrate active engagement in humanitarian coordination architecture.

sections.section_name: Coordination with national and local authorities
sections.section_label: Coordination with national and local authorities
sections.instructions: Describe coordination with national and local government authorities. Address: (1) Government engagement - coordination with relevant ministries (Interior, Social Affairs, Health, Education, etc.) and local authorities; (2) Alignment with national plans - how the action supports or aligns with national development plans, disaster management strategies, or refugee/IDP policies; (3) Capacity support - technical support or capacity building provided to government counterparts; (4) Inclusion in national frameworks - how the action is reflected in government-led coordination mechanisms or national appeals; (5) Transition planning - how activities will be handed over to government or integrated into national systems. Demonstrate respect for national sovereignty and support for government leadership in humanitarian response.

Describe coordination with development actors (World Bank, UNDP, bilateral development agencies, regional development banks). Address: (1) Development partnerships - coordination or collaboration with development organizations working in the same area/sector; (2) Nexus linkages - how this humanitarian action creates entry points for or complements development programming; (3) Joint analysis - shared assessments or planning with development actors; (4) Transition pathways - how the action facilitates transition from humanitarian to development approaches; (5) Inclusion in development frameworks - links to development plans, poverty reduction strategies, or Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Demonstrate strategic thinking about humanitarian-development nexus and longer-term solutions beyond immediate humanitarian response.

sections.fixed_text: Describe compulsory display of ECHO/EU identity at all project sites and activities. Attach evidence as required.

sections.section_name: Compulsory display of EU Humanitarian Aid visual identity on
sections.section_label: 12.1.1. Compulsory display of EU Humanitarian Aid visual identity on
sections.instructions: Confirm compliance with all 5 mandatory EU Humanitarian Aid visual identity requirements, or provide justification for any exemptions. Address each point: (A1) Building signage - EU/ECHO logos displayed on partner office buildings, health centers, distribution points, and other project facilities; (A2) Equipment - branding on vehicles, water tanks, containers, generators, and other visible equipment; (A3) Shipments and distributed goods - EU/ECHO visibility on blankets, sacks, tents, buckets, hygiene kits, cash/debit cards, and all items distributed to beneficiaries; (A4) Operational/outreach materials - branding on training materials, flyers, notebooks, posters, and beneficiary-facing communications; (A5) Staff clothing - EU/ECHO logos on T-shirts, field vests, caps, and other items worn by project staff. For each requirement, confirm implementation or provide detailed justification if exemption is needed (e.g., security concerns, cultural sensitivities, operational constraints). Attach photos or evidence as required.

sections.section_name: External communication of EU funding and partnership
sections.section_label: 12.1.2. External communication of EU funding and partnership
sections.fixed_text: To ensure visibility of ECHO's contribution, UNHCR will ensure ECHO's logo is included in UNHCR's multi-donor banners and other regular externally available publications, both in Arabic and English. Specific mentions to ECHO will be made on social media networks in accordance with UNHCR's internal policy on donor visibility. ECHO's logo will be placed in project locations funded through this contribution if security conditions permit. UNHCR will also remain available to facilitate meetings upon request of ECHO. ECHO's contribution will be further acknowledged in UNHCR's regular situation updates. In order to make communication and visibility activities as adequate as possible to respond to ECHO's expectations, UNHCR will liaise with ECHO Regional Information Office (RIO) and ECHO field office to discuss frequency, scope, schedule and channels to use for these activities. In Brussels, UNHCR visibility & communication team will be in regular contact with its ECHO counterparts to optimize the use of the outputs stemming from visibility & communication activities.

sections.section_name: Do you foresee communication actions that go beyond standard obligations?
sections.section_label: 12.2. Do you foresee communication actions that go beyond standard obligations?
sections.instructions: YES Above-standard, visibility, information and communication activities - /NO: Standard visibility, information and communication activities . If YES, describe above-standard plans and submit communication plan : Specify how the communication action contributes to raising public awareness of and support for the EU and its humanitarian work and how it complements other communication actions by your organisation and/or by the European Commission
Enter eligible direct cost figures as per submitted budget.

sections.section_name: Percentage of direct eligible costs allocated to the support costs
sections.section_label: 13.2. Percentage of direct eligible costs allocated to the support costs
sections.instructions: Enter allowed percentage (≤7%) for indirect/support costs.

sections.section_label: 13.3. Funding of the action - We fill this part for you based on your planned budget
sections.instructions: Enter breakdown: ECHO, self, other donors, beneficiary, and revenue contributions. Table must align with annexed budget.

sections.section_label: 13.4. Explanation about 100% funding - N/A. All ECHO proposals are multi-donor
sections.instructions: Explain rationale if requesting 100% funding; otherwise N/A for multi-donor.

sections.section_name: Annex financial statement and detailed budget
sections.section_label: 13.5. You are expected to upload to APPEL the annex with financial statement and detailed budget
sections.instructions: Upload latest Excel/financial annex as required.
sections.fixed_text: Upload the latest financial statement and detailed budget annex separately in APPEL as required. No narrative needed in this field.

sections.instructions: Describe any in-kind contributions. These are not included in total funding.

sections.fixed_text: The Executive Committee of the High Commissioner's Programme annually reviews UNHCR's programs for approval. Funding, however, depends almost exclusively on voluntary contributions outside those provided through the United Nations Regular Budget. UNHCR, thus, has no guaranteed level of income to cover all its approved and mandated activities. In addition, many voluntary contributions received by UNHCR either have no earmarking or are limited to a particular region/country. Such funds are thus allocated, as and when they are pledged and received, to the different operations according to requirements. Therefore, UNHCR can only state the identity of all donors to a specific operation once its annual accounts are closed. Information on the current year is published annually with UNHCR's Global Appeal (available on UNHCR web site. This proposal is for a multi-donor action, and as such, no overlap or double funding can occur, except in the unlikely event of the operation being over-funded with earmarked contributions. Should this event take place, UNHCR will consult with ECHO an appropriate action.

sections.section_label: 13.8. VAT exemption granted (including to the implementing partners)?
sections.instructions: YES/NO/Do not know yet. If no, give details of actions taken to seek exemption.

sections.fixed_text: Only if seeking pre-defined or exceptional alternative arrangements—visibility, extreme ops, or legal condition. Explain context, attach documents.

sections.section_label: 15.1. Name and title of legal representative signing the Agreement
sections.fixed_text: State the legal representative for signing.

sections.section_label: 15.2. Name, telephone, e-mail and title of the contact persons
sections.fixed_text: List at least HQ and field contact persons with all details.

sections.instructions: Provide only additional information relevant to the appraisal of this proposal that does not fit in other sections. Keep this section concise. Use it for genuinely relevant lessons learned, recent contextual changes, sensitive implementation considerations, specific challenges requiring ECHO awareness, or brief recommendations for future programming. Do not repeat information already covered elsewhere in the proposal.

Generate a comprehensive logframe table that captures: (1) Principal Objective, (2) Specific Objective, (3) Results (outputs/services delivered), (4) Activities (up to 10 per result), (5) Pre-conditions, (6) Assumptions, (7) Risks, (8) Contingency measures. For each result/objective, list all relevant KOI/KRI indicators, their full definitions, SMART baselines, targets (with units), sources/methods for data collection, and comments (standards used, other explanations). Ensure correct hierarchy and clarity: objectives at the top (1 per action, then specific, then result level), indicators per objective/result, with full consistency and correct cross-linkages. Use SMART, gender-disaggregated, and ECHO-compliant indicators wherever possible, referencing relevant Annex SF2/SF3/SF4. Present as a well-structured markdown or spreadsheet-style table with labeled column headings for all required fields (objective/result, indicator, baseline, target, source, assumption, risk, activity, etc.).

columns.name: Level
columns.instructions: Principal Objective / Specific Objective / Result / Activity

columns.name: Indicator
columns.instructions: KOI/KRI or custom indicator name and unit

columns.name: Definition
columns.instructions: Indicator definition as per list/Annex or custom

columns.name: Contingency Measures
columns.instructions: Mitigation or contingency plan for critical risks

sections.instructions: Generate a concise checklist and summary of the required annexes for proposal submission. Cover the dedicated embedded annex sections generated below and identify whether each is mandatory, optional, fully generated in the proposal, or not applicable.  Do not leave this section blank. Keep descriptions short and functional.

columns.name: Annex Name
columns.instructions: Title/short name of annex (e.g., Budget, Workplan, Map, etc.)

columns.name: Brief Description
columns.instructions: Purpose or source/justification for the annex

columns.name: File Name
columns.instructions: Actual file name to attach or NA if not submitted

sections.instructions: Generate the detailed budget directly in this section instead of referring to an external Excel annex. Present a structured budget table covering the main direct cost categories, short description of each cost line, amount, funding source where relevant, and concise justification. Ensure consistency with sections 7.3, 13.1, 13.3, and 13.4. If exact figures are not available, state that the detailed budget parameters are not fully available in the context and provide the clearest possible structured draft without inventing unsupported amounts.

Generate the workplan directly in this section instead of referring only to an external annex. Present a Gantt-style implementation schedule using the existing table structure. Show the major activities, start period, end period, responsible lead, and key milestone or dependency. Ensure full consistency with section 7.3 Results and the logic of intervention.

Generate the location and mapping content directly in this section. If an actual map cannot be embedded, provide a clear location narrative covering intervention sites, geographic spread, admin divisions, camp/out-of-camp setting, border or access corridors where relevant, and any location details needed for appraisal. Keep it consistent with sections 2.1 and 2.2. If maps are not required or not supported by the available inputs, state Not Applicable.

sections.section_name: Embedded Partner List and Implementation Arrangements
sections.instructions: Generate a consolidated partner annex directly in this section. Include all partners and implementing partners relevant to the action and summarize their type, role, status, location or address details where available, and key implementation or capacity-building arrangements. Keep this section aligned with sections 10.6 and 10.7.

Generate the environmental screening content directly in this section when applicable. Summarize the main environmental risks linked to the action, likely impacts, minimum environmental requirements, mitigation measures, and any greening approach relevant to the intervention. Keep it consistent with sections 4.3, 7.5, 7.6, and 8.

Generate the resilience-related annex content directly in this section when applicable. Summarize risk-informed programming, vulnerability analysis, do-no-harm considerations, local capacity strengthening, and how the action contributes to resilience over time. Keep it aligned with section 8.

Generate the protection, accountability, safeguarding, AGD, PSEA, and data-protection-related supporting content directly in this section when applicable. Summarize key protection mainstreaming measures, safeguarding controls, referral pathways, complaint handling, and inclusion measures. Keep it consistent with sections 4, 5, 6, and 9.

Generate the humanitarian-development-peace nexus and sustainability supporting content directly in this section when applicable. Summarize transition strategy, development linkages, national-system alignment, sustainability measures, and exit or handover considerations. Keep it aligned with sections 8 and 11.

Generate the capacity-building annex content directly in this section when applicable. Describe the training, mentoring, institutional support, monitoring follow-up, and expected capacity outcomes for local authorities, partners, community structures, or affected populations. Keep it aligned with sections 8, 10.6, and 10.7.

Generate a summary of the assessment reports directly in this section whenever assessments are referenced in the proposal. Summarize each key assessment, date, lead agency, methodology, relevance, and core findings that support the action. Keep it fully consistent with section 4.1.

Generate the evaluation or learning plan directly in this section when relevant. If no evaluation, audit, or learning exercise is planned, write Not Applicable. If relevant, summarize purpose, timing, scope, methodology, intended use of findings, and relation to section 9.4.

sections.section_name: Embedded TORs for External Evaluations and Audits
sections.instructions: Generate concise Terms of Reference content directly in this section only when an external evaluation or audit is funded under the action. If not applicable, write Not Applicable. If applicable, include purpose, scope, key questions, deliverables, timing, and reporting line, consistent with section 9.4.

Generate the VAT-related supporting information directly in this section. State the VAT exemption status, any pending actions to secure exemption, and any implications for implementing partners if relevant. Keep it aligned with section 13.8. If not applicable or unknown, state this clearly.

Generate the indirect cost explanation directly in this section. Summarize the rationale for the support-cost percentage used, how indirect costs relate to the action, and any donor-specific compliance points relevant to the support-cost level. Keep it consistent with section 13.2.

Generate a fallback plan for co-funding directly in this section whenever relevant. Explain how the action would be adapted, phased, prioritized, or supplemented if expected complementary funding is delayed, reduced, or not confirmed. Keep it aligned with sections 13.3, 13.4, and 13.7. If not relevant, write Not Applicable.

sections.section_label: 17.16. Additional Documents Required by Sector Guidance
sections.instructions: Generate any additional supporting content required by sector guidance directly in this section when relevant. Summarize what sector-specific supporting notes, compliance details, technical standards, or methodological clarifications are required for the action and provide the content directly here. If no additional sector-guidance documents are relevant, write Not Applicable.
